narrative: As an Asian male applicant with an upper-middle-class family background, your law school application was evaluated using a model trained on data from thousands of students who took the bar exam, predicting their likelihood of passing on the first try. The model assessed your profile— including your undergraduate GPA of 3.30, LSAT score of 36.50, Year 1 school decile ranking of 3rd out of 10, Year 3 decile of 2nd out of 10, and plans to attend full-time—and calculated a 52.7% probability that you would fail the bar exam. This tipped the scales just over the threshold, leading to the rejection of your application as the school prioritizes candidates predicted to pass with high confidence. The most influential factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 decile ranking, which placed you in the bottom 20% of your school that year, signaling to the model a significant dip in academic performance compared to peers. Next in importance was your undergraduate GPA, which actually worked in your favor by being slightly above the typical 3.21 average, pulling the prediction toward success and softening the overall risk. Your Year 1 decile ranking, landing in the lower third of your school (below the average 6th decile), also contributed toward the failure prediction, though less strongly than your later performance. While your LSAT was nearly spot-on with the average and your full-time status aligns with most students, these didn't outweigh the concerns from your decile rankings. In comparison to typical students who pass, your profile shows stronger undergrad grades but weaker school rankings, especially in Year 3, which the model sees as a red flag for bar exam readiness. This isn't a judgment on your potential as an Asian male from a supportive background—many in similar situations succeed with targeted improvements. To move forward, focus on boosting your academic deciles through stronger coursework or retakes, perhaps retaking the LSAT if feasible, and consider part-time options or gap-year experiences that demonstrate upward trends. Gathering letters of recommendation highlighting your resilience could also help reframe your story for other schools. With these steps, you can build a profile that reassures admissions models and committees alike. Keep pushing—you've already shown GPA strength that sets you apart.
